title_en: Ibn Qudamah al-Maqdisi: Life and Scholarly Formation
title_ar: Ibn Qudamah al-Maqdisi: Life and Scholarly Formation
title_id: Ibn Qudamah al-Maqdisi: Kehidupan dan Pembentukan Keilmuannya
content_id:
Muwaffaq ad-Din Abdullah ibn Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Qudamah al-Maqdisi lahir pada 541 H (1147 M) di desa Jammain dekat Nablus di Palestina, di kawasan yang secara historis dikenal sebagai wilayah Maqdisi atau Yerusalem. Keluarganya termasuk di antara ulama Hanbali yang telah melestarikan tradisi Ahmad ibn Hanbal di Levant, dan ia tumbuh terbenam dalam kehidupan keagamaan dan keilmuan komunitas itu. Ketika pendudukan Salib membuat kehidupan semakin sulit, keluarganya berhijrah ke Damaskus, di mana ia akan menghabiskan sebagian besar hidupnya dan menjadi ulama Hanbali terkemuka di eranya.

Di Damaskus, Ibn Qudamah belajar di bawah para master Hanbali terkemuka, termasuk sepupunya yaitu ahli fikih dan ulama hadis Abd al-Ghani al-Maqdisi. Ia juga melakukan perjalanan ke Baghdad, pusat historis mazhab Hanbali, di mana ia menghabiskan beberapa tahun belajar dengan otoritas-otoritas Hanbali terbesar di zamannya, termasuk Syaikh Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani yang terkenal. Tahun-tahun di Baghdad memberinya akses ke tradisi manuskrip fikih Hanbali yang paling tua dan paling otoritatif serta memperdalam penguasaannya atas fikih dan hadis.

Ibn Qudamah juga seorang pria yang aktif. Ia berpartisipasi dalam jihad melawan Tentara Salib di bawah Shalah ad-Din (Saladin), menyaksikan pembebasan Yerusalem pada 583 H (1187 M). Keterlibatan dengan realitas politik dan militer zamannya ini tidak mengalihkan perhatiannya dari keilmuan; bahkan jika ada sesuatu, hal itu memberi tulisan hukumnya kualitas yang membumi -- kepedulian terhadap masalah-masalah nyata yang dihadapi Muslim nyata dalam keadaan yang menantang.

Ia wafat di Damaskus pada 620 H (1223 M), meninggalkan warisan fikih Hanbali yang paling komprehensif yang dihasilkan oleh satu pengarang tunggal. Karya-karyanya berkisar dari primer bagi pemula hingga mahakarya ensiklopedis, dan tetap menjadi perpustakaan rujukan inti mazhab Hanbali hingga hari ini. Pembentukannya membentuk fikih Hanbali selama berabad-abad.
content_en:
Muwaffaq ad-Din Abdullah ibn Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Qudamah al-Maqdisi was born in 541 AH (1147 CE) in the village of Jammain near Nablus in Palestine, in the region historically known as the Maqdisi or Jerusalem district. His family were among the Hanbali scholars who had preserved the tradition of Ahmad ibn Hanbal in the Levant, and he grew up immersed in the religious and scholarly life of that community. When the Crusader occupation made life increasingly difficult, his family emigrated to Damascus, where he would spend the greater portion of his life and become the preeminent Hanbali scholar of his era.

In Damascus, Ibn Qudamah studied under leading Hanbali masters, including his cousin the jurist and hadith scholar Abd al-Ghani al-Maqdisi. He also traveled to Baghdad, the historical center of the Hanbali school, where he spent several years studying with the greatest Hanbali authorities of his time, including the celebrated Shaykh Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani. These years in Baghdad gave him access to the oldest and most authoritative manuscript traditions of Hanbali jurisprudence and deepened his mastery of both fiqh and hadith.

Ibn Qudamah was also a man of action. He participated in the jihad against the Crusaders under Salah ad-Din (Saladin), witnessing the liberation of Jerusalem in 583 AH (1187 CE). This engagement with the political and military realities of his time did not distract from his scholarship; if anything, it gave his legal writing a grounded quality — a concern with real problems faced by real Muslims in challenging circumstances.

He died in Damascus in 620 AH (1223 CE), leaving behind the most comprehensive body of Hanbali jurisprudence produced by any single author. His works range from beginner's primers to encyclopedic masterpieces, and they remain the core reference library of the Hanbali madhab to this day. His formation shaped Hanbali jurisprudence for centuries.
